heading: APPARATI
card_summary: Chunk 146 opens the Apparati (scholarly apparatus) of Campanini's 2010 Bompiani edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, presenting the initial portion of the critical bibliography (entries A–C, spanning Abrams 1996 through Campanini 1999d). The bibliography records scholarship on Christian Kabbalah, Venetian humanism, Jewish-Christian intellectual exchange, Hebrew grammar, and Renaissance philosophy, with recurring entries by Saverio Campanini himself on Zorzi's Hebrew sources, the symbolic structure of the De Harmonia Mundi, and figures such as Reuchlin, Soncino, and Ovadyah Sforno. Entries cover fields ranging from Keplerian harmony theory (Aiton-Duncan-Field 1997) and Plotinian scholarship (Brisson 1992) to Franciscan preaching in Venice (Berengo Morte 1946) and the Venetian Accademia of Francesco Patrizi da Cherso (Bolzoni 1980–1995). The list also indexes key scholarship on Giulio Camillo's Theatre of Memory, Pico della Mirandola's Heptaplus, and the diffusion of Kabbalah in English literature (Blau 1942–1944).
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 146 constitutes the opening section of the *Apparati* (scholarly apparatus) appended to Saverio Campanini's Italian critical edition of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Bompiani, 2010). The apparatus is organized into four components: (I) Bibliography, (II) Index of Hebrew terms, (III) Index of Greek terms, and (IV) Index of biblical passages. This chunk presents the bibliography from entries beginning with "A" through the final entries under "C" (Campanini 1999d), covering pages 2814–2819 of the critical edition (pagination reflected in section headers visible in the chunk at pp. 2814 and 2816 and 2818).

## Structure of the Bibliography

The bibliography is organized in the standard European critical-edition format: author surname followed by year of publication, then full bibliographic data. Entries are alphabetical and include monographs, edited volumes, journal articles, and collective volumes spanning the late nineteenth century through 2010. The bibliography is notably international, drawing on Italian, German, English, French, and Hebrew scholarship.

## Saverio Campanini's Own Scholarship as Index of the Edition's Concerns

A striking feature of the bibliography is the density of entries authored by Campanini himself, which collectively constitute a scholarly autobiography and indicate the intellectual lineage of the edition:

- **Campanini 1997**: "La struttura simbolica del *De Harmonia Mundi* di Francesco Zorzi," published in *Materia Giudaica* 3 (1997), pp. 13–17. The title — *Haophan betoc haophan* ("wheel within the wheel," Ezekiel 1:16) — signals Zorzi's Kabbalistic-Ezekielian interpretive framework as the structural key to the entire work.

- **Campanini 1999**: "Le fonti ebraiche del *De Harmonia mundi* di Francesco Zorzi," in *Annali di Ca' Foscari* XXXVIII, 3 (1999), pp. 29–74. This article is the cornerstone of the edition, establishing the specific Hebrew and Kabbalistic textual sources underlying Zorzi's synthesis.

- **Campanini 1996**: On Ovadyah Sforno and his relations with Christians, situating one of Zorzi's likely interlocutors within the broader context of Jewish-Christian intellectual exchange in sixteenth-century Italy.

- **Campanini 1998**: "Considerazioni sulla qabbalah cristiana e la nascita della giudaistica moderna," tracing Christian Kabbalah's role in the emergence of modern Jewish studies.

These entries, taken together, reveal that Campanini approached the *De Harmonia Mundi* as a document of Christian Kabbalah that must be decoded through its Hebrew sources — a methodological commitment that distinguishes his edition from earlier approaches.

## Christian Kabbalah and Renaissance Humanism

Several clusters of entries define the intellectual terrain mapped by the edition:

### On Christian Kabbalah
- **Blau 1942** and **Blau 1944** (*The Christian Interpretation of the Cabala in the Renaissance*, Columbia University Press) — the foundational English-language survey of the field, here cited in its Italian translation (*Le origini della Cabala*, Controluce, Nardò 2010).
- **Busi 1997a**: "Francesco Zorzi. A Methodical Dreamer," in Dan 1997, pp. 97–125 — a direct characterization of Zorzi's kabbalistic method.
- **Busi 1998**: *La Qabbalah* (Laterza) — the standard Italian introduction.
- **Busi–Campanini 1995**: The Italian translation of Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*, which Campanini co-edited, establishing his credentials in the Reuchlinian tradition directly relevant to Zorzi.

### On the Venetian Intellectual Context
- **Albasini 1952**: "La biblioteca di S. Francesco della Vigna in Venezia" — the library of Zorzi's own convent, whose holdings were foundational for his synthesis.
- **Berengo Morte 1946**: Franciscan preachers at the Basilica of S. Marco, documenting the milieu of Observant Franciscan preaching in Venice from which Zorzi emerged.
- **Bassi 1985**: The collected architectural treatises including Francesco Giorgi (Zorzi)'s memorandum on the design of S. Francesco della Vigna — the famous *parere* of 1535 in which Zorzi applied harmonic principles from the *De Harmonia Mundi* to sacred architecture.
- **Bedouelle–Le Gal 1987**: *Le «divorce» du roi Henry VIII* (Droz) — documenting Zorzi's role as expert consultant in the annulment controversy, a key episode in his career.

### On the Theatre of Memory and Giulio Camillo
A substantial cluster of entries by Lina Bolzoni (1974, 1980, 1981, 1981b, 1983, 1984, 1991, 1995) and Corrado Bologna (1988, 1991) addresses Giulio Camillo's *Teatro della Memoria*, a Venetian contemporary of Zorzi whose encyclopedic mnemonic project shared conceptual affinities with the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s ambition to map all knowledge onto a harmonic-Kabbalistic grid.

### On Pico della Mirandola
- **Black 2006**: *Pico's Heptaplus and Biblical Hermeneutics* (Brill) — situating Zorzi's hermeneutics within the Pican tradition of Kabbalistic biblical exegesis.

### On Johannes Reuchlin
Multiple Campanini entries (1995, 1996b, 1999a, 1999c) track Zorzi's relationship to Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*, establishing the German Christian Kabbalist as a proximate model for Zorzi's method.

## Kepler and Musical Harmony

The entry **Aiton–Duncan–Field 1997** (*Kepler, The Harmony of the World*, Diane Publishing) marks the edition's awareness of the long arc from Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (1525) to Kepler's *Harmonice Mundi* (1619), both of which sought mathematical-harmonic principles underlying cosmic order.

## Hebrew and Jewish Scholarship

The bibliography reflects the edition's commitment to reading Zorzi through his Jewish sources:

- **Abrams 1996**: On R. Asher ben David's Kabbalistic thought (Cherub Press) — situating one strand of early Kabbalah relevant to Zorzi's sources.
- **Busi 2001**: *Catalogue of the Kabbalistic Manuscripts in the Library of the Jewish Community of Mantua* — the manuscript tradition in which Zorzi's Hebrew sources circulated.
- **Busi–Bondoni–Campanini 2004**: *The Great Parchment. Flavius Mithridates' Latin Translation* — on the crucial intermediary figure of Flavius Mithridates, through whom Kabbalistic texts became available to Pico and subsequently to Zorzi.
- **Campanini 1997b**: "Peculium Abrae. La grammatica ebraico-latina di Avraham de Balmes" — on the Hebrew grammarian whose work was directly used by Zorzi.
- **Altmann–Stern 1958**: *Isaac Israeli. A neoplatonic philosopher* (Oxford) — on the Neoplatonic Jewish philosopher whose synthesis of Plotinian and Hebrew ideas prefigures aspects of Zorzi's own project.

## Gilles de Viterbo and the Roman Context

- **Astruc–Monfrin 1961**: "Livres latins et hébreux du Cardinal Gilles de Viterbe" (*Bibliothèque d'Humanisme et Renaissance* 23, 1961) — on Cardinal Egidio da Viterbo, the Augustinian theologian who was one of the most important patrons of Christian Kabbalah in Rome and a significant influence on Zorzi's milieu.

## Methodological Significance

The bibliography as a whole encodes the methodological commitments of Campanini's edition: to read Zorzi not as an isolated Venetian mystic but as a figure at the intersection of Observant Franciscan theology, Venetian civic humanism, Christian Kabbalah in the tradition of Pico and Reuchlin, and the broader Renaissance recovery of Hebrew learning. The density of Campanini's own prior publications — particularly his 1999 article on Zorzi's Hebrew sources — signals that the edition is the culmination of a sustained research program rather than a single editorial act.

The entry **Brucker 1766** (*Historia critica philosophiae*, Leipzig) represents the earliest piece of secondary literature cited, marking the beginning of the historiography of Zorzi as a philosopher of Renaissance syncretism.

## Representative Bibliographic Entries

> **CAMPANINI 1997** S. Campanini, *Haophan betoc haophan*. La struttura simbolica del *De Harmonia Mundi* di Francesco Zorzi, in «Materia Giudaica» 3 (1997), pp. 13–17.
> <cite>Chunk 146, p. 2817</cite>

> **BUSI 1997a** G. Busi, Francesco Zorzi. A Methodical Dreamer, in DAN 1997, pp. 97–125.
> <cite>Chunk 146, p. 2815</cite>

> **CAMPANINI 1999** S. Campanini, Le fonti ebraiche del *De Harmonia mundi* di Francesco Zorzi, in «Annali di Ca' Foscari», XXXVIII, 3 (1999), pp. 29–74.
> <cite>Chunk 146, p. 2818</cite>

> **BEDOUELLE – LE GAL 1987** G. Bedouelle – P. Le Gal, Le «divorce» du roi Henry VIII, Droz, Genève 1987.
> <cite>Chunk 146, p. 2814</cite>
